title: Heart conditions, palpitations and high blood pressure
summary: Several cold medicines can increase heart rate or blood pressure, disturb sleep or worsen fluid retention. Saline, rest and carefully selected single-ingredient medicines are usually safer starting points.
evidenceSnapshot: Pseudoephedrine and similar sympathomimetics can cause cardiovascular stimulation. NSAIDs can also be problematic in hypertension, kidney disease or heart failure, so pharmacist or clinician review is worthwhile.

interventions.name: Saline spray or rinse
interventions.finding: Can reduce congestion without systemic stimulant effects.
interventions.practical: Use commercial saline or sterile/distilled/boiled-and-cooled water for irrigation.
interventions.cautions: Use gentle pressure; ask for advice after sinus surgery or with severe ear problems.

interventions.name: Paracetamol for pain or fever
interventions.finding: Provides symptom relief without the vasoconstrictor effect of decongestants.
interventions.practical: Follow the label and check combinations for duplicate paracetamol.
interventions.cautions: Liver toxicity with overdose; may not suit everyone.

interventions.name: Pseudoephedrine and oral sympathomimetics
interventions.finding: Can improve congestion but may raise heart rate or blood pressure and trigger palpitations, tremor or insomnia.
interventions.practical: Discuss with a pharmacist before use; avoid when contraindicated.
interventions.cautions: Particular caution with uncontrolled hypertension, arrhythmia, coronary disease, hyperthyroidism and interacting medicines.

interventions.name: Medicated nasal decongestant sprays
interventions.finding: Work locally and can relieve congestion rapidly, but systemic absorption and rebound congestion remain possible.
interventions.practical: Use only for the short label duration.
interventions.cautions: Ask before use with significant cardiovascular disease; stop if palpitations or severe headache occur.

interventions.name: Ibuprofen and other NSAIDs
interventions.finding: Relieve pain and fever but can raise blood pressure, affect kidney function or worsen fluid retention in susceptible people.
interventions.practical: Use only when suitable and for the shortest time.
interventions.cautions: Extra caution with heart failure, kidney disease, anticoagulants, ulcers or dehydration.

practicalSteps: Choose a single active ingredient for one symptom.
Check labels for pseudoephedrine, phenylephrine, ibuprofen and hidden caffeine.
Keep taking prescribed heart medicines unless the treating clinician advises otherwise.
Ask a pharmacist to screen for interactions, including MAO inhibitors and other stimulants.
avoidOrCare: Avoid oral phenylephrine as an assumed safer/effective substitute; evidence for oral efficacy is poor and sympathomimetic effects remain possible.
Avoid combining decongestants or taking them near bedtime.
Avoid NSAIDs during dehydration or a heart-failure flare unless advised.

myths.claim: A nasal medicine cannot affect the heart.
myths.reality: Some medicated sprays can be absorbed and may still cause cardiovascular effects.

myths.claim: Phenylephrine is a reliable non-prescription substitute for pseudoephedrine.
myths.reality: The FDA's evidence review found oral phenylephrine ineffective at marketed doses.

redFlags: Chest pain, fainting, sustained rapid or irregular heartbeat, severe headache or neurological symptoms
New or worsening breathlessness, ankle swelling or sudden weight gain in heart failure
Blood pressure markedly above the person's urgent-action threshold
Symptoms after taking a decongestant that do not settle promptly
unsettled: Cardiovascular risk varies with dose, formulation and the person's condition; product labels cannot replace an individual medication review.
